meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in wildlife pressure and camp consequence

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Black Bear
research_grounding.use: Black bear range, omnivorous behavior, forest/chaparral habitat, food conflict, and livestock/camp pressure.

image_prompt.subject: fresh black bear tracks and a torn flour sack beside a wrecked camp box, with a cinnamon-colored black bear half-visible in brush beyond
image_prompt.scene: early morning pine camp; cold ashes, scattered beans, claw marks on a log, a tense horse at frame edge, and bedrolls pulled back from the mess
image_prompt.composition: square clue scene with tracks leading from foreground evidence to the partly hidden animal; the mistake is clear before the bear is
image_prompt.camera: low ground-level inspection angle, bear visible but not lunging, like a PS3-era in-engine tracking moment
material_cues: horse tack pulled tight by nervous movement
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Let the animal be ordinary and therefore frightening. The camp made the invitation.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine wildlife clue scene
render_traits: slightly simplified bear, horse, and camp-object silhouettes
modest texture resolution on fur, canvas, mud, ash, and pine
hard ambient occlusion under camp box, logs, and the bear's brush cover
cold morning light with damp ground highlights
believable 1899 camp gear and food storage
naturalistic animal pressure rather than monster staging
style_modifiers.palette: cold ash gray, pine green, mud brown, flour white, cinnamon bear fur, canvas tan, wet grass green, and weak dawn blue.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable labels, brands, signs, crate markings, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The tracks, torn sack, camp box, bear, and nervous horse are fully readable.
Every visible prop belongs plausibly to 1899 camp life.
The bear is naturalistic and the camp mistake is visible.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
